definition.name: Barvinok_Pataki_bound_classical
definition.definition: (Classical Barvinok-Pataki bound). Consider the semidefinite program: minimize ⟨C, X⟩ subject to ⟨A_j, X⟩ = b_j (j = 1,...,m) and X ≽ 0, where X ranges over real symmetric (or Hermitian) n×n matrices. If X̄ is an extreme point of the feasible set with rank(X̄) = r, then: (Real case) r(r+1)/2 ≤ m; equivalently r ≤ ⌊(√(8m+1) - 1)/2⌋. (Complex/Hermitian case) r² ≤ m; equivalently r ≤ ⌊√m⌋. These bounds are tight. The proof proceeds by showing that dim(face(X̄, S^n_+)) ≤ m (Pataki) or equivalently by a tangent space dimension count (Barvinok). The real/complex distinction is critical: real symmetric matrices have dim = n(n+1)/2, while Hermitian matrices have dim = n². Our abstract theorem naturally gives the complex version r² ≤ m+1 (with the +1 from trace normalisation, which in the standard SDP formulation is counted among the m constraints). References: Barvinok, Discrete Comput. Geom. 25, 2001; Pataki, Math. Oper. Res. 23(2), 1998.
